claim: Agentic AI development progresses through six levels: spicy autocomplete -> pair programmer -> code generator -> manager-reviewer -> delegator -> autonomous factory (dark factory)
actionable: Map your current AI-assisted development to one of six autonomy levels; use this as a roadmap to gradually increase automation as models improve
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Most teams adopting AI tools are at level 2-3 (pair programmer / code reviewer) where they manually direct agents and review outputs
actionable: When adopting AI coding tools, expect to start at pair programming level; plan multi-quarter roadmap to reach higher autonomy tiers
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Codebase structure (modularity, co-location) directly impacts agent discoverability - agents find relevant files faster in modular codebases with well-grouped code
actionable: Before enabling autonomous agents, refactor codebase for modularity and co-location so agents can discover context within localized folders instead of searching entire repo
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Usage patterns (e.g., standard auth methods, startup scripts, test patterns) should be documented and co-located so agents can reference and replicate them
actionable: Create reference files (README, patterns/) documenting your auth, test, and startup approaches; agents will use these as templates for new code
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Cursor rules should emerge dynamically based on observed agent failures (e.g., 'agents keep touching auth code incorrectly, add a rule'), not installed wholesale from templates
actionable: Don't bulk-install all recommended cursor rules at project start; instead, create rules iteratively as you observe agents going off-rails in specific ways
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: As models improve, they follow rules and guardrails better - agents are less likely to go off-rails, suggesting guardrails matter less with future model versions
actionable: Reassess and lighten guardrails periodically as you upgrade to newer model versions; rules that prevented issues in older models may become unnecessary
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Agents must be able to verify their own work (unit tests, integration tests, UI/e2e tests) to enable autonomous operation - testing is a prerequisite for factory scaling
actionable: Before scaling agents, add unit, integration, and e2e tests (UI testing via browser control); agents should run and validate tests themselves before human review
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Enabling agents to start and control development environments (running package.json scripts, starting servers) at scale is possible with cloud VMs per agent
actionable: Deploy cloud agents with isolated VMs so each agent can independently start dev environments, run tests, and verify work without contention
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Agents are in-distribution for common patterns (e.g., looking at package.json for JS start scripts); priming the codebase with recognizable patterns accelerates agent adoption
actionable: Follow common conventions (package.json scripts, README.md, standard test runners) so agents quickly understand your codebase structure without additional prompting
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Backend systems with clear contracts and boundaries are easier for autonomous agents to work on than frontends requiring UI verification and visual fidelity checks
actionable: When assigning autonomous agent work, prioritize backend systems first; defer complex UI work until agents have reliable UI testing and visual verification tools
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Building verifiable systems (with testable outputs) is as important as code generation - agents need self-verification capability to work reliably without continuous human oversight
actionable: Invest equally in test infrastructure and observability as in agent prompts; agents that can't verify their work can't scale
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Feature flags enable safe autonomous agent deployment - agents can commit changes, merge PRs, and flag features as off, allowing rollback if issues arise
actionable: Give autonomous agents a skill to add feature flags to their changes; deploy flagged features off by default, enabling quick rollback if agents break things
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor

claim: Cursor 3 redesign dropped VS Code in favor of agent-first workflow - the UI/UX changes reflect that AI agents, not humans, are the primary user
actionable: When designing tools for agentic AI workflows, optimize for agent parsing and context-discovery, not human ergonomics
source_title: Building your own software factory — Eric Zakariasson, Cursor
